findings.summary: Several handler functions use generic 'handle' prefix without domain specificity, while nearby siblings use more descriptive names
evidence: GroupManager.jsx line 245: 'handleSave' — ambiguous when the component manages both create and update operations; 'handleGroupSave' or 'saveGroup' would disambiguate in stack traces
GroupManager.jsx line 263: 'handleDelete' — same issue, 'confirmAndDeleteGroup' would capture the confirm-gate behavior
GroupsPage.jsx has the opposite pattern done well: 'handleGroupToggle', 'handleSelectConfig', 'handleSaveConfig' are all domain-specific
findings.suggestion: Align GroupManager's internal handler names with the domain-specific naming pattern already established in GroupsPage. Use 'saveGroupForm' / 'confirmDeleteGroup' style names.

findings.summary: filterByRange compares date strings lexicographically, which works for ISO format but is a subtle correctness dependency with no guardrail
evidence: chartUtils.jsx line 63: 'return data.filter((d) => d.date >= cutoffStr)' — relies on YYYY-MM-DD string comparison being equivalent to chronological order
No comment or assertion documents this assumption; if a non-ISO date format ever enters the pipeline the filter silently produces wrong results
findings.suggestion: Add a brief inline comment noting the ISO-format prerequisite, or parse both sides to Date objects for an explicit numeric comparison. The performance difference is negligible for the data sizes involved.

findings.summary: Several production components lack PropTypes validation. While PropTypes have been added to key components (AccountsBreakdown, GroupsSnapshot, GroupSnapshotControls, SyncControl), many others still have none.
evidence: StatsCards.jsx: no PropTypes on the default export despite receiving a complex 'stats' object with nested 'current', 'mom', 'yoy' properties
NetWorthChart.jsx: no PropTypes on 'history' prop (expects an array of { date, net_worth, assets, liabilities })
BudgetChart.jsx: no PropTypes despite three props including the complex 'totalsByMonth' map
BudgetTable.jsx: no PropTypes despite complex 'categories' array-of-objects prop
SyncHistory.jsx, SyncJobStatus.jsx: no PropTypes despite complex job/history data shapes
RangeSelector.jsx: no PropTypes despite being a reusable shared component
GroupManager.jsx: no PropTypes on the main export or its sub-components (GroupForm, GroupList)
findings.suggestion: Add PropTypes to all production components that accept props. This is especially important for the shared/reusable components (RangeSelector, BudgetChart, BudgetTable) and page-level components that receive complex data shapes. Consider migrating to TypeScript for stronger guarantees.

findings.summary: GroupsPage bypasses the named API module exports, calling fetchJSON and postJSON directly with raw URL strings instead of using the domain-specific wrappers in api.js
evidence: GroupsPage.jsx line 7: 'import { fetchJSON, postJSON } from '../api.js'' — imports the low-level primitives
GroupsPage.jsx line 22: 'fetchJSON('/api/accounts/summary')' — should use fetchAccountsSummary()

GroupsPage.jsx line 30-33: 'fetchJSON('/api/groups'), fetchJSON('/api/groups/history'), fetchJSON('/api/groups/snapshot'), fetchJSON('/api/groups/configs')' — the first three have named wrappers (fetchGroups, fetchGroupsHistory, fetchGroupsSnapshot) that should be used, the last (/api/groups/configs) has no wrapper at all
GroupsPage.jsx line 98: 'postJSON('/api/groups/configs', ...)' — used in multiple places with no wrapper
All other pages (BudgetPage, SyncPage, SetupPage, App) consistently use the named api.js exports
findings.suggestion: Add fetchGroupsConfigs() and saveGroupsConfigs() wrappers to api.js, then update GroupsPage to import only named domain functions. This maintains the single-source-of-truth pattern established by api.js and matches the pattern used by all other pages.

findings.summary: Error handling across pages and components uses inconsistent strategies for surfacing errors to users — some use state-driven error displays, others use console.warn fire-and-forget
evidence: GroupsPage.jsx line 98: 'postJSON(...).catch((err) => console.warn('Failed to persist active config:', err))' — config save failures are silently swallowed from the user's perspective
GroupsPage.jsx lines 107-108: 'console.warn('Failed to save config:', err)' — same pattern for save/delete/rename config operations
GroupManager.jsx line 256-258: handleSave properly shows formError on failure — the right approach
GroupManager.jsx line 269-271: handleDelete properly shows listError on failure
App.jsx line 54: setError(err.message) — proper error surfacing for dashboard data fetch
findings.suggestion: Replace the console.warn catch handlers in GroupsPage's config operations with a user-visible toast or inline error message. The user should know when their saved view configurations fail to persist. Even a transient notification would be better than silent failure.

findings.summary: GroupsSnapshot receives 11 props and passes 10 of them straight through to GroupSnapshotControls — a prop-drilling pass-through pattern
evidence: GroupsSnapshot.jsx lines 57-69: receives groups, selectedGroupIds, configs, activeConfigId, conflictMap, onGroupToggle, onSelectConfig, onSaveConfig, onDeleteConfig, onRenameConfig — then passes all of them to GroupSnapshotControls at lines 110-121
GroupsPage.jsx could render GroupSnapshotControls directly alongside GroupsSnapshot, avoiding the pass-through
findings.suggestion: Consider rendering GroupSnapshotControls directly in GroupsPage (or as a sibling to GroupsSnapshot) rather than routing all control props through GroupsSnapshot. GroupsSnapshot's core responsibility is displaying the chart and summary table, not relaying control callbacks.

findings.summary: Multiple components define nearly identical tooltip style objects, a pattern suggestive of copy-paste across feature additions
evidence: chartUtils.jsx line 97-104: exports TOOLTIP_STYLE with background '#1e2130', border '1px solid #2d3348', borderRadius 6, etc.
NetWorthChart.jsx line 12: defines local tooltipStyles.wrap with the same background and border values but borderRadius 8
GroupsSnapshot.jsx line 19-24: defines local tooltipStyles.wrap with same values, borderRadius 8
GroupsTimeChart.jsx line 10: spreads TOOLTIP_STYLE then overrides borderRadius to 8 and padding — partial reuse
BudgetChart.jsx line 12: correctly uses TOOLTIP_STYLE directly without redefinition
findings.suggestion: Standardize on the shared TOOLTIP_STYLE from chartUtils.jsx. If 8px border radius is preferred, update the constant once. NetWorthChart and GroupsSnapshot should spread TOOLTIP_STYLE with any overrides rather than redefining the same values.

findings.summary: chartUtils.jsx combines formatting functions, chart constants, sync-related duration helpers, and JSX chart element factories — it serves too many domains
evidence: chartUtils.jsx exports 20+ symbols spanning: currency formatting (fmtCompact, fmtFull), date formatting (formatDateLabel, fmtBudgetMonth, fmtDatetime, fmtDatetimeSecs), data transformation (filterByRange, downsample), sync duration helpers (durationFinal, durationElapsed, formatSeconds), chart constants (AXIS_TICK, GRID_STROKE, TOOLTIP_STYLE, COLOR_*), range options (COMMON_RANGES), and JSX factory (sharedChartElements)
Duration helpers (durationFinal, durationElapsed) are only used by sync components (SyncHistory, SyncJobStatus), not by chart components
fmtDatetime and fmtDatetimeSecs are also only used by sync components
findings.suggestion: Extract sync-specific formatters (fmtDatetime, fmtDatetimeSecs, durationFinal, durationElapsed) into a separate syncUtils.js file in the constants or components directory. chartUtils should only contain chart-related formatting and constants.

findings.summary: GroupsPage manages 8 state variables and 5 callback handlers, performing orchestration that could benefit from custom hook extraction
evidence: GroupsPage.jsx lines 9-17: 8 useState calls for groups, accounts, historyData, snapshot, configs, activeConfigId, selectedGroupIds, loading, error
Lines 85-132: 5 useCallback handlers for toggle, select, save, delete, rename configs — each with their own async fetch-and-setState logic
Lines 56-75: conflictMap computation is a derived-state useMemo unrelated to rendering
The component is 191 lines, making it the longest file in the codebase alongside being the most stateful
findings.suggestion: Extract the config management state and handlers into a useGroupConfigs() custom hook (managing configs, activeConfigId, save/delete/rename handlers). This would reduce GroupsPage to orchestration of three hooks: data loading, config management, and selection state.

findings.summary: BudgetTable defines its own fmtDollar function that largely duplicates fmtFull from chartUtils, differing only in parenthetical notation for negatives
evidence: BudgetTable.jsx lines 5-9: defines fmtDollar(n) that formats currency with parentheses for negatives
chartUtils.jsx lines 14-21: fmtFull(n) uses Intl.NumberFormat for currency formatting
Both handle null with '—', both format USD, the only semantic difference is negative formatting (parens vs minus sign)
findings.suggestion: Either add a fmtAccounting variant to chartUtils.jsx that uses sign: 'accounting' in Intl.NumberFormat options, or have fmtDollar call fmtFull internally and wrap negatives in parens. This avoids maintaining two independent currency formatters.

findings.summary: GroupsPage imports fetchJSON/postJSON primitives instead of domain wrappers, creating a backdoor around the api.js module boundary
evidence: GroupsPage.jsx line 7: imports fetchJSON and postJSON directly from api.js
GroupsPage.jsx uses raw URL strings '/api/groups/configs' in 4 places — this endpoint has no api.js wrapper
All other page components (BudgetPage, SyncPage, SetupPage, App) exclusively import named domain functions from api.js
This means a URL change to the configs endpoint requires modifying GroupsPage.jsx instead of just api.js
findings.suggestion: Add fetchGroupsConfigs() and saveGroupsConfigs() to api.js and update GroupsPage to use them. This closes the gap in the api module boundary.

findings.summary: syncEntities.js creates module-level constant objects; this is fine for constants but worth noting as the only non-trivial module-scope initialization
evidence: syncEntities.js creates 5 exported constant objects at module scope — all pure data with no I/O or side effects
No circular dependencies, no import-order sensitivity detected
findings.suggestion: No action needed. The module-scope initialization is appropriate for pure constants. Score reflects the clean absence of initialization coupling issues across the codebase.

findings.summary: Import statements inconsistently include or omit .jsx/.js file extensions, and some imports omit extensions entirely (relying on Vite resolution)
evidence: GroupsPage.jsx line 3: 'import GroupsTimeChart from '../components/GroupsTimeChart'' — no extension
GroupsPage.jsx line 4: 'import GroupsSnapshot from '../components/GroupsSnapshot'' — no extension
GroupsPage.jsx line 5: 'import GroupManager from '../components/GroupManager'' — no extension
GroupsPage.jsx line 7: 'import { fetchJSON, postJSON } from '../api.js'' — has .js extension
GroupsSnapshot.jsx line 14: 'import { useResponsive } from '../hooks/useResponsive'' — no extension
Meanwhile App.jsx, BudgetPage.jsx, SyncPage.jsx consistently include extensions on all imports
findings.suggestion: Standardize on always including the file extension in imports, matching the majority convention in the codebase. This is also more robust for ESM module resolution.

findings.summary: Some components use CSS variables for colors while inline styles in the same file use raw hex values, despite comments acknowledging the pattern
evidence: StatsCards.jsx lines 9,12,22: uses raw hex '#34d399' and '#f87171' for arrow colors inline, while CSS module files use CSS variables for the same semantic colors
chartUtils.jsx lines 107-110: exports COLOR_POSITIVE='#34d399' and COLOR_NEGATIVE='#f87171' as named constants — StatsCards should import these instead of duplicating the literals
SyncHistory.jsx line 58: uses SYNC_STATUS_ICON color (CSS vars) correctly — good pattern
findings.suggestion: Import COLOR_POSITIVE and COLOR_NEGATIVE from chartUtils in StatsCards.jsx rather than hardcoding the same hex values. This ensures color consistency if the palette is ever updated.

findings.summary: prop-types is a production dependency but only provides development-time warnings; in production builds it becomes dead weight unless stripped
evidence: package.json line 14: 'prop-types': '^15.8.1' listed under dependencies (not devDependencies)
PropTypes checks are stripped in production React builds, but the prop-types library itself is still bundled unless tree-shaken
findings.suggestion: This is a minor concern since Vite's production build will tree-shake unused code. If bundle size becomes a concern, consider moving to TypeScript instead. No immediate action needed.

findings.summary: RangeSelector (shared reusable component) and AutoSyncSettings have adequate test coverage, but BudgetTable's tests only check rendering — no tests verify the fmtDollar formatter or the SummaryTable computation logic
evidence: BudgetTable.jsx lines 5-9: fmtDollar function has distinct formatting logic (parenthetical negatives, Math.abs) that is untested in isolation
BudgetTable.jsx lines 58-124: SummaryTable performs non-trivial income/expense/net calculations across months — no unit test verifies the actual computed numbers, only that rows render
The existing BudgetTable tests are rendering-only: they verify text presence but never assert computed dollar amounts
findings.suggestion: Add unit tests for fmtDollar (especially negative formatting and null handling) and add a test that verifies SummaryTable's computed net values against expected numbers from the mock data.

findings.summary: GroupsPage's config persistence handlers (save, delete, rename, select) use fire-and-forget patterns with console.warn on failure — these failure paths are not tested
evidence: GroupsPage.jsx lines 94-132: handleSelectConfig, handleSaveConfig, handleDeleteConfig, handleRenameConfig all have catch blocks that console.warn but no error state — untested
GroupsPage.test.jsx: no test simulates a failing POST to /api/groups/configs
By contrast, GroupManager.test.jsx does test delete failure (line 129-138)
findings.suggestion: Add tests that mock /api/groups/configs to fail and verify the console.warn is called (or, better, add user-visible error handling and test that).

findings.summary: GroupsSnapshot accepts 11 props, most of which are pass-throughs for its child GroupSnapshotControls — the component's API surface is wider than its actual responsibility
evidence: GroupsSnapshot.jsx lines 57-69: accepts snapshot, groups, selectedGroupIds, configs, activeConfigId, conflictMap, onGroupToggle, onSelectConfig, onSaveConfig, onDeleteConfig, onRenameConfig
Of these 11 props, only 'snapshot' is used by GroupsSnapshot's own rendering logic (chart + table). The other 10 are forwarded to GroupSnapshotControls
findings.suggestion: Either flatten the component hierarchy so GroupsPage renders GroupSnapshotControls directly, or bundle the control-related props into a single 'controlProps' object to make the pass-through explicit and reduce the apparent surface area.

findings.summary: The app uses a configured flag rather than session auth, relying on localhost-only access — this is documented but the security boundary is implicit
evidence: App.jsx lines 31-34: Comment explicitly documents the localhost-only security model and notes that token/session auth should be added if ever exposed beyond localhost
The setup check at lines 36-39 determines access based solely on /api/setup/status configured flag
findings.suggestion: The existing documentation comment is good. No action needed unless the deployment model changes. Score reflects the deliberate, documented design choice.

findings.summary: GroupsPage still uses raw fetchJSON/postJSON while all other pages have been migrated to named API function exports — this is an incomplete migration of the api.js module pattern
evidence: api.js defines named exports for groups: fetchGroups, fetchGroupsHistory, fetchGroupsSnapshot (lines 31-33)
GroupsPage.jsx line 22: uses fetchJSON('/api/accounts/summary') instead of fetchAccountsSummary()
GroupsPage.jsx lines 30-33: uses fetchJSON('/api/groups') instead of fetchGroups(), etc.
GroupsPage.jsx lines 98, 105, 116, 125: uses postJSON('/api/groups/configs', ...) — no wrapper exists in api.js for this endpoint
BudgetPage.jsx, SyncPage.jsx, App.jsx all consistently use the named API exports
findings.suggestion: Complete the migration: (1) add fetchGroupsConfigs() and saveGroupsConfigs() to api.js, (2) update GroupsPage to import and use all named API functions instead of fetchJSON/postJSON.

findings.summary: chartUtils.jsx lives in components/ but is a utility module, not a component — its placement is inconsistent with the directory's purpose
evidence: components/ directory contains 15 .jsx component files and 1 utility file (chartUtils.jsx)
constants/ directory already exists and holds syncEntities.js — chartUtils' constants and formatters would fit there, or in a new utils/ directory
chartUtils.jsx is imported by 8 different files including components and pages — it is a cross-cutting utility
findings.suggestion: Move chartUtils.jsx to a utils/ directory (or split into constants/chartConstants.js and utils/formatters.js). This better reflects its role as a shared utility rather than a component.

findings.summary: GroupsPage combines data fetching, config management, conflict computation, and selection state into a single 191-line component with 8 state variables
evidence: GroupsPage.jsx: 8 useState hooks, 1 useMemo for conflictMap, 1 useMemo for filteredSnapshot, 5 useCallback handlers

Three distinct responsibilities: (1) data loading (groups, accounts, history, snapshot), (2) config CRUD (save, delete, rename, select), (3) selection state management (selectedGroupIds, conflictMap, filteredSnapshot)
Compare with SyncPage.jsx (72 lines, 5 state variables) and BudgetPage.jsx (85 lines, 4 state variables)
findings.suggestion: Extract config management into a useGroupConfigs() hook and consider extracting conflict/selection logic into useGroupSelection(). This would reduce GroupsPage to ~60 lines of composition, matching the complexity of sibling pages.

findings.summary: AccountSection in AccountsBreakdown takes 9 parameters passed individually, where a config/dimensions object would reduce the surface
evidence: AccountsBreakdown.jsx line 80: 'function AccountSection({ label, total, totalColor, pieData, groups, colors, pieHeight, pieInnerRadius, pieOuterRadius })' — 9 destructured props
Three of these (pieHeight, pieInnerRadius, pieOuterRadius) are always passed together and always derived from isMobile — they are a cohesive group
findings.suggestion: Bundle the three pie dimension props into a single 'pieDimensions' object: { height, innerRadius, outerRadius }. This reduces the parameter count and makes the coupling between the three values explicit.
